sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 3-month history of cough, initially noticed during horse loading, which has been intermittent and resolved with a cold, but has recurred.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, well-controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and has been a former smoker for 7 years.
The patient exercises regularly, including biking, and has no history of lung infections or pneumonia.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, well-controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking, which the patient stopped 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies pain, shortness of breath, or any other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilateral, the heart has a regular rate and rhythm, no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, and pulses are intact in all extremities.
The skin is pink, warm, and dry, with no edema to the lower extremities.
There is some swelling to the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: A chest CT scan shows a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; Smoking history.
Plan:
I ordered a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for lung function and a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.

I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which involves three small incisions on the side of the right chest, under general anesthesia, and the patient will be admitted for one night and discharged the following day.
The patient will have a chest tube until the following day and will be taken out at bedside.
I also recommend that the patient continues to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will see me again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
